test.name: Subject-as-child census — creates sibling person stubs in tree.gedcomx.json
test.description: When the subject (Patrick Flynn) appears as a child in an 1850 census household, the record-extraction skill must do TWO things: (a) extract the per-person assertions as usual, AND (b) create minimal person stubs in tree.gedcomx.json for the subject's siblings on this record (Bridget, John), with ParentChild relationships linking each sibling to the existing parent (Thomas Flynn, I2). The mid-research-flynn scenario seeds Patrick (I1) and Thomas (I2) — plus Mary (I3) and James (I4), prior-research children documented from St. Mary's baptismal registers (R2/R3 cite S5, not this census) — so the trigger condition (existing in-tree parent) is satisfied AND the test's siblings Bridget/John are NOT in the tree yet, which lets us exercise the write path. Mary and James do NOT appear on this census record: the record-vs-tree children discrepancy is an identity question the skill must surface, never a data error to 'fix'. The correct persistence path is ONE tree_edit({operation: 'add_household_children', parents: [...], children: [...]}) call listing the record's household roster — the tool matches Thomas against the tree, skips Patrick (already a child of I2), creates the Bridget/John stubs with ParentChild edges to Thomas, and returns a checklist the agent relays. The subject's own person + Patrick→Thomas ParentChild already exist (this is mid-research) and must NOT be re-created. This unblocks relativesChildBirthRange40 and downstream skills end-to-end.

input.user_message:
search-records found this 1850 U.S. Census record where Patrick is a child in Thomas Flynn's household. Extract assertions for everyone and make sure the tree reflects the siblings.

1850 United States Census — Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania
FamilySearch ark:/61903/1:1:MXYZ-TP4 · Dwelling 84, Family 91

| Name | Age | Sex | Occupation | Birthplace |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Thomas Flynn | 32 | M | Laborer | Ireland |
| Bridget Flynn | 7 | F |  | Ireland |
| Patrick Flynn | 5 | M |  | Ireland |
| John Flynn | 2 | M |  | Pennsylvania |

judge_context: Should extract per-person assertions for every household member (Thomas, Bridget, Patrick, John) — name, age/birth, birthplace at minimum — and write them via research_append. This is unchanged behavior from prior extractions and must continue to work

Should detect that the subject Patrick is in a child role (child_2 or similar) and that his parent Thomas already exists in tree.gedcomx.json as I2 — this is the trigger condition for sibling stub creation per SKILL.md §5d

Should make ONE tree_edit({operation: 'add_household_children', parents: [{given, surname, gender}...], children: [{given, surname, gender}...]}) call listing the record's household roster (Thomas as parent; Bridget, Patrick, John as children) and relay the tool's returned checklist (parentsMatched/created/skipped/edgesAdded). The tool — not the agent — matches parents, dedups children, creates the Bridget/John stubs (e.g. I5 and I6) and the ParentChild edges to Thomas; the agent never passes ids and never pre-enumerates the tree. (A run that instead persists the stubs via separate add_person + add_relationship tree_edit ops still reaches the correct end state but misses the single-call contract — grade the persistence-path dimension down, not the outcome.)

The end state must show new person stubs for Bridget Flynn (Female) and John Flynn (Male) with a ParentChild edge from Thomas (I2) to each — created by the tool from the household call, two edges total

Should NOT create a new person for Patrick — Patrick already exists in the tree as I1 (listing Patrick among children[] is correct; the tool skips him as already_child_of_parent). Should NOT recreate the Thomas→Patrick ParentChild edge (R1 already exists). Duplicating either in the end state would be an error

DO NOT score Correctness or Tool Arguments down for including Patrick in the add_household_children children[] array. The extractor has NO tree-read tool and is explicitly instructed to pass the full household roster (the subject included) and to never pre-check the tree; the tool's `already_child_of_parent` skip is the intended idempotent contract, not a wrong argument or a duplication. Grade the FINAL persisted state — Bridget and John stubbed with ParentChild edges to Thomas, no Patrick duplicate, no duplicate R1 edge — not which names appeared in the call. A call of children=[Bridget, Patrick, John] and a call of children=[Bridget, John] are both correct here.

Mary (I3) and James (I4) are prior-research children — their ParentChild edges (R2, R3) cite the St. Mary's baptismal registers (S5), not this census — and they do NOT appear on this census record. The CORRECT behavior is BOTH stubbing Bridget and John AND flagging the record-vs-tree children discrepancy (Mary and James in the tree but absent from the 1850 household) as an identity question in the summary. RENAMING or rewriting existing persons is a FAIL — e.g. update_name/update_person turning I3 into Bridget or I4 into John, or any remove op; the tree children and the record children are different people until identity work says otherwise
Should NOT create a person stub for Thomas — Thomas is the head_of_household, not a sibling of the subject, and is already in the tree as I2

Per-sibling assertions in research.json (Bridget's name, age, birthplace; John's name, age, birthplace) remain attached to record_role child_N — they are NOT moved onto the new person stubs. The stubs carry only the minimal person shape (id assigned by tool, preferred name, gender). Detailed facts attach later via person-evidence linking these assertions to the new persons
